mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers Confucianism from its founding by Confucius through Mencius, Xunzi, Han dynasty adoption, Neo-Confucianism, and influence across East Asia — mapping comprehensively to the history and philosophy of Confucianism (B).

Directly covers Shinto including kami, rituals and shrines, Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, State Shinto and nationalism, and its interaction with Buddhism — a comprehensive match to Shinto: the Way of the Gods (E.2).

Directly covers Daoism including Dao De Jing, Zhuangzi, wu wei, religious Daoism with alchemy and immortality practices — a comprehensive match to the history, literature, and philosophy of Taoism (C).

The Warring States period with the Hundred Schools of Thought including Confucianism, Daoism, Legalism, and Mohism directly addresses the formulation of the Great Tradition (A.2.b) and Confucianism's early development (B.1.c).

The Han Synthesis blending Confucian, Legalist, and Daoist thought during the Han Dynasty directly addresses the establishment of Confucianism as state orthodoxy (B.1.d) and traditional concepts in Chinese religious thought (A.3).

Zen Buddhism's origins in Chinese Chan and its profound influence on Japanese culture (tea ceremony, calligraphy, garden design) directly addresses the introduction of Buddhism to Japan (E.1.b) and Japanese religious art and symbolism (E.3).

Romance of the Three Kingdoms covers Chinese cultural values including loyalty and statecraft during an era of Buddhist-Confucian interaction — relevant to the dominance of the Buddhist Way (A.2.c) and Confucian literature (B.2).
The samurai's connection to Zen Buddhism, tea ceremony, and the Tokugawa era addresses Tokugawa-era religious developments (E.1.c) and Japanese religious art and symbolism (E.3).
The Korean Empire covers late Joseon dynasty developments and modernisation including religious dimensions — relevant to the history of Korean religion (D.1).

mappings.episodeTitle: Sun Tzu and The Art of War
mappings.rationaleAI: Sun Tzu and The Art of War from the Warring States period relates to the formulation of Chinese intellectual traditions alongside Confucianism and Daoism (A.2.b).
